title: Cryptographic Proofs
summary: An introductory lesson on why proofs matter when a system wants to convince others that a confidential computation behaved correctly.

bodySections.title: Proofs as Evidence
bodySections.body:
Proof systems help bridge the gap between hidden inputs and public assurance. They let a system provide evidence about how a computation was performed without revealing all of the private state involved.

That is one of the core tools for making confidential execution credible to counterparties.

bodySections.title: System Trust
bodySections.body:
A system becomes easier to trust when it can show not only that a result exists, but that the result emerged from the right process.

Proofs are part of that story because they constrain what the system can claim without backing it up.

quizQuestions.prompt: What problem do proofs solve in confidential systems?
How are proofs framed in this lesson?
choices: As evidence about correct computation without full disclosure
As a replacement for all consensus
As a way to avoid any validation
